title: Turn In Work
text:
You turn in question sets and assignments in one place: the assignments repository. You are a collaborator on it, so you clone it, do your work in your own folder, push a branch, and open a pull request. A bot pre-checks the pull request and comments with a report; the instructor reviews the science and merges.
Everyone uses the same loop:

Clone the assignments repo (once) and make your folder: submissions/<your-username>/.
Start a branch and do the week’s work under submissions/<your-username>/weekNN/.
Commit your files, including a rendered report and a submission.yml manifest.
Push the branch and open a pull request into main.
Read the automatic pre-check comment and fix anything it flags.

The pull request is the receipt. The branch freezes the submitted version. The manifest tells the checker what evidence belongs to the submission.

title: Turn In Work
text:
You turn in question sets and assignments in one place: the assignments repository. You are a collaborator on it, so you clone it, do your work in your own folder, push a branch, and open a pull request. A bot pre-checks the pull request and comments with a report; the instructor reviews the science and merges.
Everyone uses the same loop:

Clone the assignments repo (once) and make your folder: submissions/<your-username>/.
Start a branch and do the week’s work under submissions/<your-username>/weekNN/.
Commit your files, including a rendered report and a submission.yml manifest.
Push the branch and open a pull request into main.
Read the automatic pre-check comment and fix anything it flags.

The pull request is the receipt. The branch freezes the submitted version. The manifest tells the checker what evidence belongs to the submission.

title: Turn In Work
text:
Where your work lives
All of your work for the whole term lives under one folder, one subfolder per week:
submissions/
  <your-username>/
    week02/
      analysis.qmd            # or answers.md for a question set
      analysis.html           # you render this yourself
      output/read-counts.csv
      output/qc-summary.png
      submission.yml          # the manifest
Only edit files inside submissions/<your-username>/. The bot fails any pull request that touches another student’s folder or the assignment prompts.

title: Turn In Work
text:
What gets checked automatically
The automatic check is not meant to replace instructor judgment. It checks the things software is good at checking:

Your pull request only touches your own folder.
A submission.yml manifest is present in the week folder you changed.
The manifest identifies the week, type, title, and submitted files.
The source file and rendered report exist and are non-empty.
Output tables and figures listed in the manifest exist and are non-empty.
Raw data are not committed under any data/raw/ directory.

The instructor still evaluates interpretation, biological reasoning, caveats, and scientific quality. The bot does not run or render your code — you commit your own rendered .html.

title: Turn In Work
text:
The manifest
Copy templates/submission.yml into your week folder and fill it in. All paths are relative to the folder that holds submission.yml (your week folder).
Question set example:
type: question-set
week: "02"
title: "Raw reads, FASTQ, QC, and trimming"
source: answers.md
rendered: answers.html
questions_answered: 5
project_connection: true
Assignment example:
type: assignment
week: "02"
title: "FASTQ Quality Control"
source: analysis.qmd
rendered: analysis.html
outputs:
  - output/read-counts.csv
  - output/qc-summary.png
uses_raw_data: true
raw_data_committed: false
project_connection: true

title: Turn In Work
text:
The automatic report
After you open or update the pull request, GitHub Actions posts a report as a comment (updated in place each time you push) like:
Automatic pre-check: warn

Passed
- All changed files are under your folder `submissions/<you>/`.
- Manifest type is `assignment`.
- Rendered file found.
- Output file found.

Warnings
- Manifest does not confirm a project connection.

Needs human review
- Biological interpretation and caveats.
The pull request also gets a label: auto:pass, auto:warn, or auto:fail, and the pull-request check turns red on fail. If it fails, fix the flagged items, commit, and push to the same branch — the pre-check runs again automatically.

title: Turn In Work
section: Why this is better than uploads or issues
text:
Why this is better than uploads or issues
This system assesses the same habits the course is trying to teach:

Version-controlled work.
Reproducible project structure.
Clear evidence trails.
Precise submission versions (the branch and its commits).
Fast feedback before grading.
Human review reserved for scientific judgment.

It also lets the instructor see cohort-level progress: open pull requests are outstanding submissions, and auto:* labels triage them at a glance.

title: Turn In Work
text:
Weekly research progress
Weekly project progress is not a pull request. It is a structured project-management checkpoint, submitted through a Weekly Research Progress issue on the course repository.
Each progress issue asks for:

Week number.
Course repository URL.
Project issue URL.
Current commit SHA or GitHub compare URL.
What you accomplished this week.
Evidence links to commits, outputs, reports, figures, Issues, or PRs.
Whether last week’s goals were met.
Goals for next week.
Self-rating: On track, Some risk, or Blocked.
Blockers or help needs.

The automatic progress check comments on the issue and applies labels:







Label
Meaning




progress:on-track
The weekly record is complete and the student reports forward motion.


progress:watch
The record is incomplete, thin, risky, or needs instructor attention.


progress:blocked
The student reports being blocked and should have a linked Help Request or Blocker/Bug issue.



The progress checker looks for completeness and evidence, not whether the science is “good enough.” The instructor uses the report to review trajectory, intervene early, and assess weekly project-progress credit.

title: Before Class — Self-Directed Setup
text: You will work across three computing platforms this quarter. Difficulty ramps up with each tier, so get a working, verified setup on all three before the first class. Each tutorial is self-paced, opens in your browser, and ends with a completion checklist.

title: Before Class — Self-Directed Setup
text:
The three tutorials



#
Tutorial
Platform
What you’ll be able to do




1
Your Computer: Shell & Git/GitHub
Your laptop/desktop
Install the tools, organize a repo, run the clone→commit→push loop


2
Raven: RStudio Server & Reproducible Analysis
Raven
Log into the lab server, run an analysis, push results


3
Hyak / Klone: HPC with SLURM & Containers
Hyak / Klone
Submit your first batch job on the cluster

title: Before Class — Self-Directed Setup
text:
After you finish
Open a Setup Check issue in your course repository (use the Setup Check issue form) and paste in the completion block from the bottom of each tutorial. This is how we confirm you’re ready — and your first taste of how the course uses GitHub Issues to track work.

title: Before Class — Self-Directed Setup
section: Why these three, and where they come from
text:
Why these three, and where they come from
Each tutorial maps to the Roberts Lab Handbook, the reference standard for how you’re expected to work:

Tutorial 1 → Computing Best Practices, Data Management, and the handbook’s Git/GitHub and bash tutorials.
Tutorial 2 → the handbook’s Raven (RStudio Server, Conda) guides.
Tutorial 3 → the handbook’s Klone guides (logging in, running a job, containers, file transfers, storage).

Keep the handbook open in a tab while you work — it’s where you’ll return all quarter.

title: Project and Compendium Rubric
text:
The project grade rewards steady, reproducible research practice as much as the final biological result. A strong project does not need to prove a perfect story. It should show clear decisions, traceable data, working code, interpretable outputs, and honest limits.
This rubric covers the project-facing parts of the course grade:








Course component
Course weight
Main evidence




Weekly Research Project Progress
20%
Weekly progress issues, project issue, linked commits, outputs, blockers, and next steps


Project Presentation
10%
Mid-quarter update and final presentation


Project Completion
10%
Week 10 final research compendium



Automatic checks confirm that required files, manifests, rendered reports, links, and commits exist. Instructor review evaluates scientific judgment, interpretation, reproducibility, and communication.

title: Project and Compendium Rubric
text:
The project grade rewards steady, reproducible research practice as much as the final biological result. A strong project does not need to prove a perfect story. It should show clear decisions, traceable data, working code, interpretable outputs, and honest limits.
This rubric covers the project-facing parts of the course grade:








Course component
Course weight
Main evidence




Weekly Research Project Progress
20%
Weekly progress issues, project issue, linked commits, outputs, blockers, and next steps


Project Presentation
10%
Mid-quarter update and final presentation


Project Completion
10%
Week 10 final research compendium



Automatic checks confirm that required files, manifests, rendered reports, links, and commits exist. Instructor review evaluates scientific judgment, interpretation, reproducibility, and communication.

title: Project and Compendium Rubric
text:
Weekly research project progress
Weekly progress is assessed from the weekly-progress issue, project issue, automatic progress report, linked commits, and linked evidence.











Criterion
Points
Excellent
Complete
Developing
Incomplete




Goals and follow-through
20
Goals are specific, realistic, and clearly connected to the final compendium; previous goals are addressed directly.
Goals are stated and mostly connected to the project; most previous goals are addressed.
Goals are vague, too broad, or weakly connected to the final product.
Goals are missing or not meaningfully updated.


Evidence of work
25
Links to commits, reports, figures, tables, logs, issues, or outputs provide concrete proof of progress.
Evidence is present and generally supports the progress claim.
Evidence is thin, hard to inspect, or only partially connected to the claim.
Evidence is missing or cannot be accessed.


Technical progress
20
Work advances the project workflow, resolves a risk, or creates a meaningful intermediate or final product.
Work shows forward movement on analysis, data organization, or documentation.
Work is mostly exploratory, disconnected, or repeated without clear learning.
Little or no project movement is visible.


Reproducible practice
15
Repository organization, commit history, file paths, and documentation make the work easy to inspect.
Most project materials are organized and version-controlled.
Organization is inconsistent, with unclear paths or stale outputs.
Work cannot be followed from the repository.


Blockers and help-seeking
10
Blockers are described precisely, with attempted fixes and linked help requests when needed.
Blockers or risks are named with enough context to respond.
Blockers are vague or raised late.
Blockers are absent despite stalled progress.


Next-week plan
10
Next steps are concrete, prioritized, and sized for one week.
Next steps are stated and plausible.
Next steps are too broad or not actionable.
Next steps are missing.

title: Project and Compendium Rubric
text:
Final compendium
The final compendium is the complete project package in the student’s repository. It should include a top-level README.md, final rendered report, source .qmd or scripts, key outputs, data availability statement, software/platform statement, and links to project and weekly-progress issues.











Criterion
Points
Excellent
Complete
Developing
Incomplete




Research question and context
10
Question is clear, biologically meaningful, and grounded in appropriate background.
Question and context are understandable.
Question is present but broad, shifting, or weakly motivated.
Question or context is missing.


Data provenance and organization
15
Data sources, sample structure, file locations, raw/processed distinction, and data availability are explicit.
Main data sources and project structure are described.
Some data details are unclear or scattered.
Data source, structure, or availability cannot be determined.


Workflow and reproducibility
20
A reader can trace the workflow from input to output; code, commands, environment notes, and render instructions are sufficient to rerun or audit the analysis.
Workflow is mostly reproducible with minor gaps.
Workflow can be partially followed but requires guessing.
Workflow is not reproducible from the repository.


Technical execution
15
Methods are appropriate for the data type and project question; QC and intermediate checks support the analysis.
Methods are generally appropriate and include some checks.
Methods are attempted but weakly justified or missing important checks.
Analysis is technically absent, broken, or inappropriate.


Results and evidence
15
Figures, tables, and summaries directly support the main result and are clearly labeled.
Results are present and mostly connected to the question.
Results are thin, hard to interpret, or disconnected from the question.
Results are missing or not inspectable.


Interpretation and biological reasoning
10
Claims are proportional to the evidence and distinguish results from interpretation.
Interpretation is reasonable but may be limited.
Interpretation overreaches, stays superficial, or misses key uncertainty.
Biological interpretation is missing.


Caveats and next steps
10
Limitations are specific, honest, and connected to concrete next analyses or data needs.
Main caveats and next steps are included.
Caveats are generic or next steps are vague.
Caveats or next steps are missing.


Communication and polish
5
Report and README are readable, well structured, linked, and free of avoidable confusion.
Materials are readable with minor rough spots.
Materials are difficult to scan or inconsistently formatted.
Materials are too incomplete or confusing to evaluate.

title: Project and Compendium Rubric
text:
Project presentation
The presentation grade includes the mid-quarter update and final presentation. The goal is to explain the project clearly enough that another student can understand the question, workflow, strongest evidence, and remaining uncertainty.











Criterion
Points
Excellent
Complete
Developing
Incomplete




Project framing
15
Research question, motivation, and data type are immediately clear.
Question and data type are understandable.
Framing is present but hard to follow.
Framing is missing.


Workflow explanation
20
Workflow is explained at the right level, with key inputs, tools, decisions, and checks.
Workflow is mostly clear.
Workflow is too vague, too detailed, or missing important steps.
Workflow cannot be understood.


Evidence and results
25
Figures or tables are legible, relevant, and support the main claim.
Results are shown and generally interpreted.
Results are present but hard to read or weakly connected to the claim.
Results are missing.


Interpretation and limits
20
Speaker clearly separates what the evidence supports from what remains uncertain.
Interpretation and at least one limitation are included.
Interpretation is superficial or caveats are generic.
Interpretation or limitations are missing.


Delivery and discussion
20
Talk is organized, fits the time, uses clear visuals, and handles questions thoughtfully.
Talk is understandable and mostly within time.
Talk is hard to follow, too rushed, or visually cluttered.
Talk is not prepared or cannot be evaluated.

title: Project and Compendium Rubric
text:
Baseline expectations
These expectations apply to all project submissions:

Do not commit large raw data files unless the instructor has explicitly approved it.
Link to exact commits, not only the repository homepage.
Keep source files and rendered outputs together or clearly linked.
Label figures and tables so they can be understood outside the live presentation.
Document software, platforms, and major package versions when they affect reproducibility.
Explain what did not work. Failed or partial analyses can still earn credit when they are documented clearly and used to motivate next steps.

title: Project and Compendium Rubric
text:
Grade interpretation
Use these ranges as a guide when translating points into feedback:







Range
Meaning




90-100
Strong, reproducible, clearly interpreted project work


80-89
Solid work with minor gaps in reproducibility, evidence, or explanation


70-79
Meaningful progress, but important pieces are unclear, incomplete, or weakly connected


60-69
Limited project evidence or substantial reproducibility and interpretation gaps


Below 60
Missing, inaccessible, or too incomplete to evaluate reliably

title: Presentations and the Last Mile
text: Week 10 turns the quarter’s work into a final research compendium and presentation. The focus is synthesis: what you did, what you found, what remains uncertain, and how someone else can inspect the work.

title: Presentations and the Last Mile
text: Week 10 turns the quarter’s work into a final research compendium and presentation. The focus is synthesis: what you did, what you found, what remains uncertain, and how someone else can inspect the work.

title: Presentations and the Last Mile
section: By the end of this week
text:
By the end of this week
You should be able to:

Present a bioinformatics project clearly.
Submit a reproducible final compendium.
Separate results from interpretation and caveats.
Archive code, outputs, and documentation.
Reflect on what you would do next with more time.

title: Presentations and the Last Mile
text:
The final compendium
Your compendium is the complete project package. It should include:

A rendered report.
Code used to produce the main results.
Key output tables and figures.
A README that explains the repository.
A note about software, platforms, and data availability.

title: Presentations and the Last Mile
text:
Presentation goals
Your final presentation should answer:

What was the research question?
What data and workflow did you use?
What is the strongest result?
What are the main limitations?
What should happen next?

title: Presentations and the Last Mile
text:
Last-mile checks
Before submission:

Render the report from a clean session.
Confirm links work.
Confirm figures are not stale screenshots.
Confirm raw data are handled according to policy.
Confirm the project issue and weekly progress issues tell the story of the work.

title: Presentations and the Last Mile
text:
References

Quarto documentation
Roberts Lab Handbook: Data Management
Course final-release assignment

title: Epigenetics and DNA Methylation
text: Week 07 introduces DNA methylation and related genomic-feature workflows. The emphasis is on interpreting methylation calls in genomic context and using genome browsers or motif tools carefully.

title: Epigenetics and DNA Methylation
text: Week 07 introduces DNA methylation and related genomic-feature workflows. The emphasis is on interpreting methylation calls in genomic context and using genome browsers or motif tools carefully.

title: Epigenetics and DNA Methylation
section: By the end of this week
text:
By the end of this week
You should be able to:

Explain what DNA methylation is and why it matters in environmental biology.
Distinguish loci, regions, motifs, and annotations.
Interpret methylation results with appropriate caveats.
Use IGV or genome-browser-style inspection to check a result.
Connect epigenetic outputs to genomic intervals.

title: Epigenetics and DNA Methylation
text:
DNA methylation in brief
DNA methylation is a chemical modification of DNA that can be associated with gene regulation, genome defense, development, and environmental response. In many analyses, the core data product is a table of methylation estimates at genomic positions or regions.

title: Epigenetics and DNA Methylation
text:
Common analysis questions
Methylation analyses often ask:

Which loci or regions differ among groups?
Are differentially methylated regions near genes or regulatory features?
Are particular sequence motifs enriched?
Do methylation patterns align with expression or phenotype?

title: Epigenetics and DNA Methylation
text:
Genome context matters
A coordinate alone is not an interpretation. You usually need annotation:

Chromosome or scaffold.
Start and end coordinate.
Strand where relevant.
Nearby genes or features.
Genome version.

title: Epigenetics and DNA Methylation
text:
Project checkpoint
Launch the structure of your final project report. It can be mostly placeholders, but every planned section should exist with notes about what evidence will go there.

title: Epigenetics and DNA Methylation
text:
References

Roberts Lab Handbook: Genomic Resources
IGV documentation
BEDtools documentation

title: Genome Annotation and Genomic Ranges
text: Week 05 gives students the coordinate logic needed across genomics: features, intervals, annotation, genome versions, and overlaps.

title: Genome Annotation and Genomic Ranges
text: Week 05 gives students the coordinate logic needed across genomics: features, intervals, annotation, genome versions, and overlaps.

title: Genome Annotation and Genomic Ranges
section: By the end of this week
text:
By the end of this week
You should be able to:

Explain BED, GFF/GTF, FASTA, BAM, and VCF at a high level.
Describe why genome build and coordinate convention matter.
Use interval logic to connect results to genes or genomic features.
Summarize overlap results without overinterpreting them.
Present a mid-quarter project update grounded in evidence.

title: Genome Annotation and Genomic Ranges
text:
Why annotation matters
Bioinformatics results often begin as identifiers or coordinates. Annotation connects those results to possible biological meaning:

Gene names and descriptions.
Functional domains.
Pathways or ontology terms.
Genomic neighborhoods.
Regulatory or repetitive features.

Annotation is useful, but it is not final proof. It gives hypotheses and context.

title: Genome Annotation and Genomic Ranges
text:
Genomic ranges
A genomic range is a chromosome/scaffold, start, end, and sometimes strand. Range operations answer questions such as:

Which variants overlap coding regions?
Which methylated regions are near genes?
Which peaks overlap promoters?
Which features are closest to my candidate loci?

title: Genome Annotation and Genomic Ranges
text:
Project checkpoint
Prepare a mid-quarter update that shows the current evidence, not only a plan. Include one concrete output, diagnostic, or figure.

title: Mapping, Assembly, and Quantification
text: Week 03 covers the central transformation in many bioinformatics workflows: reads are connected to biological references or assembled into inferred sequences, then summarized into analysis-ready tables.

title: Mapping, Assembly, and Quantification
text: Week 03 covers the central transformation in many bioinformatics workflows: reads are connected to biological references or assembled into inferred sequences, then summarized into analysis-ready tables.

title: Mapping, Assembly, and Quantification
section: By the end of this week
text:
By the end of this week
You should be able to:

Explain the difference between alignment, mapping, assembly, and quantification.
Identify when a workflow uses a reference genome, transcriptome, marker database, or no reference.
Interpret common intermediate files such as SAM/BAM, count tables, and assembly summaries.
Choose a small workflow slice that can be run reproducibly.

title: Mapping, Assembly, and Quantification
text:
Three common paths

Reference mapping
Reads are aligned to a known reference genome or transcriptome. This is common in RNA-seq, variant calling, methylation, and ChIP/ATAC-style workflows.


Assembly
Reads are assembled into longer contigs or inferred sequences when a suitable reference is absent or when the goal is to reconstruct sequence content.


Quantification
Reads are summarized into counts, abundance estimates, coverage, or feature-level tables. These tables often become the input to statistical analysis.

title: Mapping, Assembly, and Quantification
text:
The key habit
Do not skip intermediate checks. For any mapping or quantification workflow, inspect:

Number of input reads.
Number or proportion mapped.
Output file size and format.
A small preview of the result.
Logs or summary reports.

title: Mapping, Assembly, and Quantification
text:
Project checkpoint
Choose the smallest meaningful slice of your project workflow. Run it from raw or prepared input to one intermediate product, then document what the output means.

title: Getting Started
text: Week 01 turns setup into practice. You will use command-line tools, web resources, GitHub, and NCBI BLAST to answer a small biological question and begin framing your quarter project.

title: Getting Started
text: Week 01 turns setup into practice. You will use command-line tools, web resources, GitHub, and NCBI BLAST to answer a small biological question and begin framing your quarter project.

title: Getting Started
section: By the end of this week
text:
By the end of this week
You should be able to:

Explain what a FASTA file is.
Run or interpret a BLAST search.
Capture commands and results in a reproducible record.
Open a project-proposal issue with a research question, data source, platform, and endpoint.
Use Issues for help and project tracking.

title: Getting Started
text:
Biological sequence files
Many bioinformatics workflows begin with plain-text sequence files. FASTA is one of the most common formats:
>sequence_id optional description
ATGCTAGCTAGCTACGATCG
The header identifies the sequence. The following lines contain the sequence itself. Simple formats are powerful because they can be inspected, searched, counted, and transformed with many tools.

title: Getting Started
section: BLAST as a first bioinformatics workflow
text:
BLAST as a first bioinformatics workflow
BLAST compares a query sequence against a database and reports similar sequences. It is often used to ask:

What organism or gene does this sequence resemble?
Is this sequence likely contaminated?
Does this marker identify the expected taxonomic group?
Which reference sequence should I use next?

BLAST results require judgment. A low e-value helps, but interpretation also depends on query coverage, percent identity, database choice, and whether the top hits make biological sense.

Aquatic and fisheries examples
BLAST is often the first check in aquatic genomics because many projects begin with sequences from non-model species, mixed communities, or incomplete reference resources.








Scenario
Query
Useful question




Fish tissue ID
Mitochondrial COI barcode
Does this sample match the expected species or a close congener?


eDNA monitoring
12S or 16S amplicon sequence
Does the marker support a fish, invertebrate, microbial, or contaminant assignment?


Shellfish stress transcriptomics
Transcript or predicted protein
What gene family or pathway might this differentially expressed sequence represent?


Disease or parasite screening
Unknown contig from a metagenomic assembly
Does the sequence resemble host, pathogen, symbiont, or lab contamination?


Conservation genomics
Flanking sequence near a marker or variant
Is the sequence in a known gene, repeat, mitochondrial region, or poorly annotated region?





Database choice is a biological decision
There is no single best BLAST database. The right choice depends on the marker, organism, and question.

Use nucleotide databases such as NCBI nt when you have DNA and need broad taxonomic context.
Use curated resources such as RefSeq when you want fewer redundant or poorly annotated records.
Use barcode or marker-focused resources, such as BOLD for COI or a curated 12S database, when the marker has strong taxonomic conventions.
Use protein databases with blastx when a transcript or contig may encode a protein but the nucleotide sequence is too divergent for a clean nucleotide hit.
Record the database name and search date because databases change.



A misleading top-hit example
Imagine a short 12S eDNA sequence from a marine water sample. The top BLAST hit is “Atlantic salmon” with 100% identity, but the query covers only 72 bp, the same sequence also matches several salmonids, and salmon DNA is common in local hatchery, aquaculture, or food-processing contexts. The safest interpretation is not “Atlantic salmon detected.” A better interpretation is “this short marker supports a salmonid-like assignment; species-level confidence is limited by marker length, shared haplotypes, database coverage, and local contamination risk.”


BLAST interpretation checklist
For every BLAST result you use as evidence, record:

Query file and sequence length.
BLAST program used, such as blastn, blastx, or web BLAST.
Database searched and search date.
Top hit accession, description, and organism.
Percent identity, query coverage, alignment length, and e-value.
Whether other top hits suggest the same biological interpretation.
A caveat that explains what the search can and cannot support.

title: Getting Started
section: GitHub Issues as the course notebook
text:
GitHub Issues as the course notebook
This course uses Issues for structured work:

Project proposal: one home-base issue for your quarter project.
Weekly progress: what you completed, what slipped, what comes next.
Help request: what failed, where, and what you already tried.
Bug/blocker: a reproducible failure with commands and output.

title: Getting Started
text:
Project checkpoint
Open your project-proposal issue. At this stage, the proposal can be rough, but it must include:

A research question or objective.
The data you expect to use.
The likely project track.
The compute platform needed for the heavy work.
A draft endpoint: the figure, table, or result that would make the project feel complete.

Treat this first proposal as provisional. You are choosing a plausible starting point, not signing a contract with your Week 1 self.
Examples of strong draft endpoints:

RNA-seq / differential expression: a figure showing sample relationships plus a table of genes or pathways associated with temperature, salinity, disease, treatment, or life stage.
eDNA / metabarcoding: an occurrence or relative-abundance figure comparing taxa among sites, dates, depths, or treatments, with contamination caveats.
DNA methylation / epigenetics: a genomic-region summary or browser view connecting methylation differences to annotated genes or regulatory context.
Variants / population signals: a filtered variant summary, PCA, relatedness plot, or population differentiation table tied to a fisheries or conservation question.
Annotation / sequence discovery: an annotated set of genes, contigs, markers, or BLAST-supported candidates with clear evidence and limits.

title: Getting Started
text:
References

NCBI BLAST documentation
Roberts Lab Handbook: Bioinformatics Basics
Bioinformatics Data Skills, early chapters on project organization

title: Bioinformatics for Environmental Sciences
text:
This course teaches graduate students how to move from biological sequence data to defensible environmental bioinformatics interpretation. Each student develops and completes a research project targeting a reproducible final compendium by the end of the quarter. The weekly topics emphasize bioinformatics data types and analyses; computing tools such as RStudio, Quarto, GitHub, Raven, Hyak, and archiving are handled through self-directed support modules and used throughout the quarter.







Do this before Week 1



Complete the three self-directed setup tutorials on the Before Class page — one for each computing platform you will use (your own computer, Raven, and Hyak). Then open your Setup Check issue. You cannot keep up in Week 1 without a working setup on all three.


Bioinformatics is not only running tools. It is the practice of keeping data provenance clear, choosing appropriate analyses, checking intermediate products, and making biological claims that the evidence can support.

title: Bioinformatics for Environmental Sciences
text:
This course teaches graduate students how to move from biological sequence data to defensible environmental bioinformatics interpretation. Each student develops and completes a research project targeting a reproducible final compendium by the end of the quarter. The weekly topics emphasize bioinformatics data types and analyses; computing tools such as RStudio, Quarto, GitHub, Raven, Hyak, and archiving are handled through self-directed support modules and used throughout the quarter.







Do this before Week 1



Complete the three self-directed setup tutorials on the Before Class page — one for each computing platform you will use (your own computer, Raven, and Hyak). Then open your Setup Check issue. You cannot keep up in Week 1 without a working setup on all three.


Bioinformatics is not only running tools. It is the practice of keeping data provenance clear, choosing appropriate analyses, checking intermediate products, and making biological claims that the evidence can support.

title: Bioinformatics for Environmental Sciences
text:
Format
This class is driven in part by student needs and is flexible in content. It is practical and problem-driven. Tuesdays focus on the week’s bioinformatics concept and data type. Thursdays are working sessions for applying that concept to assignments and research projects.






Important



Pull requests and Issues are how we work. You turn in question sets and assignments as pull requests in the assignments repository — a bot pre-checks each one (how to turn in). Project tracking happens in GitHub Issues using course issue forms — project proposals, weekly progress, and help requests. Open-ended conversation happens in GitHub Discussions and on Slack. Be sure you have a GitHub account, are part of the course organization, and have notifications turned on.

title: Bioinformatics for Environmental Sciences
text:
Platforms
You will work across three computing tiers. Each has a dedicated setup tutorial, and the operational details live on the Self-Directed Skills page.



Tier
Platform
Access




Your computer

title: Bioinformatics for Environmental Sciences
text:
Textbook
Bioinformatics Data Skills — Reproducible and Robust Research with Open Source Tools, by Vince Buffalo (O’Reilly, 2015). Supplementary repo.

title: Bioinformatics for Environmental Sciences
text:
Grading
Each week has a bioinformatics assignment, expected research-project progress, and a question set. You will give a mid-quarter project update and a final compendium presentation. Question sets are based on the week’s reading/material and are due by 3:00pm Tuesday - review the weekly topic before class.








Assignments
Grade %
comment




Weekly Question Set
25%
due Tuesday 3:00pm


Weekly Class Assignment
35%
due Friday 5:00pm


Weekly Research Project Progress
20%
assessed via your weekly-progress issues


Project Presentation
10%
mid-quarter update + final presentation


Project Completion
10%
Week 10: compendium

title: Bioinformatics for Environmental Sciences
text:
Submitting work

Question sets → work in your folder in the assignments repository and open a pull request; a bot pre-checks it and comments.
Class assignments → same repository and pull-request flow, with a rendered report, output files, and a submission.yml manifest in your week folder.
Project progress → assessed from your weekly-progress GitHub issues and their automatic progress reports (goals set vs. met, evidence linked, blockers addressed, best practices applied).

See Turn In Work for the submission contract and automatic assessment report format. See Project and Compendium Rubric for the project-progress, presentation, and final-compendium criteria.
Late policy: 40% deduction; not accepted more than 5 days past due.

title: Bioinformatics for Environmental Sciences
text:
Core competencies
Skills in this course are anchored to the Roberts Lab Handbook — its computing best practices, data management, and platform guides (Raven, Klone) are the reference standard for how you are expected to work.

title: Bioinformatics for Environmental Sciences
text:
Weekly arc
The course moves from sequence identification and raw sequencing data toward multi-assay environmental interpretation:

Sequence databases and BLAST.
Raw reads, FASTQ, quality control, and trimming.
Mapping, alignment, assembly, and quantification.
RNA-seq and differential expression.
Genome annotation and genomic ranges.
Variants and population signals.
DNA methylation and regulatory genomics.
eDNA and metabarcoding.
Project synthesis and biological interpretation.
Final compendium.

title: About
text:
FISH 546 — Bioinformatics for Environmental Sciences — University of Washington, School of Aquatic and Fishery Sciences. Taught by Steven Roberts (Roberts Lab).
This site is built with Quarto and published via GitHub Pages. Source: https://github.com/course-fish546-2026.

title: eDNA & Environmental Metabarcoding
text: Environmental DNA (eDNA) — genetic material shed into water, soil, or air — lets us detect and characterize communities without capturing organisms. This is a growing thread in the course, with its own lecture, assignment, and an optional project track.

title: eDNA & Environmental Metabarcoding
text:
Module materials

Lecture: eDNA & Metabarcoding
Assignment: eDNA Metabarcoding pipeline
Question set: Week 08

title: eDNA & Environmental Metabarcoding
text:
What you’ll learn

Concepts: what environmental nucleic acids are, how samples are collected and filtered, and why contamination control matters.
Markers: common metabarcoding markers (COI, 12S, 16S, 18S) and when to use each.
From reads to biology: amplicon QC → DADA2 (amplicon sequence variants) vs. OTU clustering → taxonomic assignment against reference databases → an abundance / occurrence table.
Ecological readouts: diversity, species detection and occupancy, and comparing communities across sites or time.
Caveats: false positives/negatives, index hopping, and reproducibility concerns specific to eDNA.

title: eDNA & Environmental Metabarcoding
text:
Where it runs
The heavy steps (denoising, taxonomic assignment) run on Hyak/Klone — this is exactly why you complete the Hyak tutorial before class. Interactive exploration and figures happen on Raven or your own machine.

title: eDNA & Environmental Metabarcoding
text:
eDNA project track
You may take an eDNA dataset as your quarter research project: raw amplicons → community table → figure/report, using the same GitHub, issue-tracking, and compendium workflow as every other project. Flag this in your project-proposal issue.

title: Preparing for Class
text:
Week 00 makes sure every student starts with the same working habits: a GitHub repository, a sensible project layout, a terminal they can use, and a first record of their setup in GitHub Issues.
The goal is not speed. The goal is to make your work legible to yourself, your future collaborators, and the instructor.

title: Preparing for Class
text:
Week 00 makes sure every student starts with the same working habits: a GitHub repository, a sensible project layout, a terminal they can use, and a first record of their setup in GitHub Issues.
The goal is not speed. The goal is to make your work legible to yourself, your future collaborators, and the instructor.

title: Preparing for Class
section: By the end of this week
text:
By the end of this week
You should be able to:

Navigate your computer with the shell.
Create and explain a code/, data/, output/, docs/, and notebooks/ project layout.
Clone, edit, commit, and push to GitHub.
Open a GitHub Issue with enough context that someone else can help you.
Describe which tasks belong on your computer, Raven, and Hyak.

title: Preparing for Class
text:
Core habits

Work in a repository
Every course artifact should live in your course repository unless there is a clear reason it should not. Raw or very large data are usually recorded but not committed.


Make directories explain themselves
Each major directory should contain a short README.md explaining what belongs there. This prevents the common end-of-quarter problem where final-final2-new/ becomes the most important directory in the project.


Commit small, useful checkpoints
A commit should capture a meaningful step: a script added, a result regenerated, an assignment completed, or a figure revised. Commit messages should tell the next reader what changed and why.

title: Preparing for Class
text:
Project setup checklist
Create this structure in your repository:
mkdir -p code data/raw data/processed output docs notebooks assignment homework
touch code/README.md data/README.md output/README.md docs/README.md
Add data/raw/ to .gitignore if it will contain large or restricted files.

title: Preparing for Class
text:
Project checkpoint
Open your setup issue after the three pre-course tutorials are complete. Then make sure your repository has:

A clear top-level README.md.
The standard project directories.
At least one pushed commit after setup.
A note about any setup problems you still have.

title: Preparing for Class
text:
References

Roberts Lab Handbook: Computing Best Practices
Roberts Lab Handbook: Data Management
Happy Git with R

title: Raw Reads, FASTQ, QC, and Trimming
text: Week 02 starts where most sequencing projects actually start: raw reads. Before differential expression, metabarcoding, variant calling, or methylation analysis, you need to know what FASTQ files contain and whether the data are fit for the next step.

title: Raw Reads, FASTQ, QC, and Trimming
text: Week 02 starts where most sequencing projects actually start: raw reads. Before differential expression, metabarcoding, variant calling, or methylation analysis, you need to know what FASTQ files contain and whether the data are fit for the next step.

title: Raw Reads, FASTQ, QC, and Trimming
section: By the end of this week
text:
By the end of this week
You should be able to:

Explain the structure of FASTQ records.
Distinguish read counts, base quality, adapter contamination, and duplication.
Run or interpret FastQC/MultiQC-style summaries.
Decide whether trimming is justified.
Record raw-data provenance and checksums.

title: Raw Reads, FASTQ, QC, and Trimming
text:
FASTQ records
A FASTQ record contains four lines:
@read_id
ACGTTGCA
+
FFFFFFFF
The sequence line stores bases. The quality line stores per-base confidence scores encoded as characters. Most downstream workflows assume these records are correctly paired, named, and formatted.

title: Raw Reads, FASTQ, QC, and Trimming
text:
Common QC questions
Ask these before analysis:

How many reads are in each sample?
Are read qualities acceptable across the full read length?
Are adapters or primers present?
Do paired files have matching read counts?
Are there unexpected overrepresented sequences?
Are any samples obvious outliers?

title: Raw Reads, FASTQ, QC, and Trimming
text:
Trimming is a decision
Trimming can remove adapters, primers, and low-quality sequence, but it can also discard useful data. Record the exact command, software version, and parameters. If you trim, compare before and after QC summaries.

title: Raw Reads, FASTQ, QC, and Trimming
text:
Project checkpoint
For your project, identify the raw file format, source, expected sample structure, and first QC step. Add this to your project issue or README.

title: Raw Reads, FASTQ, QC, and Trimming
text:
Self-directed tools
Use the Self-Directed Skills page when you need help with Raven, Quarto rendering, Hyak jobs, or GitHub Issues.

title: RNA-seq and Differential Expression
text: Week 04 focuses on RNA-seq as a full bioinformatics workflow: raw reads become expression estimates, expression estimates become model outputs, and model outputs become biological claims.

title: RNA-seq and Differential Expression
text: Week 04 focuses on RNA-seq as a full bioinformatics workflow: raw reads become expression estimates, expression estimates become model outputs, and model outputs become biological claims.

title: RNA-seq and Differential Expression
section: By the end of this week
text:
By the end of this week
You should be able to:

Describe the RNA-seq workflow from FASTQ to count matrix.
Explain why experimental design and metadata matter.
Interpret differential-expression output cautiously.
Connect expression changes to annotation and biological context.
Identify batch effects, outliers, and weak designs.

title: RNA-seq and Differential Expression
text:
The RNA-seq data products
Common products include:

FASTQ files: raw reads.
QC reports: read quality and sample diagnostics.
BAM files or pseudoalignment outputs: read placement or transcript assignment.
Count matrix: samples by genes/transcripts.
Differential-expression table: model results.
Figures: PCA, MA plot, heatmap, volcano plot, or focused gene plot.

title: RNA-seq and Differential Expression
text:
Differential expression is a model
A differential-expression table is not a list of truths. It is evidence from a model. Interpret with:

Effect size.
Adjusted p-value or false-discovery rate.
Sample size.
Biological function.
Consistency with QC and exploratory plots.

title: RNA-seq and Differential Expression
text:
Project checkpoint
If your project is not RNA-seq, use this week to compare your own workflow to RNA-seq: what is your raw input, analysis-ready table, model or summary step, and final biological interpretation?

title: Variants and Population Signals
text: Week 06 introduces variant data and the kinds of biological questions they can support, from individual genotypes to population structure and candidate loci.

title: Variants and Population Signals
text: Week 06 introduces variant data and the kinds of biological questions they can support, from individual genotypes to population structure and candidate loci.

title: Variants and Population Signals
section: By the end of this week
text:
By the end of this week
You should be able to:

Explain what a variant call represents.
Read the major parts of a VCF file.
Distinguish SNPs, indels, genotypes, depth, and quality.
Summarize variant data by sample, region, or annotation.
Describe why filtering choices affect biological conclusions.

title: Variants and Population Signals
text:
Variant calling in context
Variant workflows typically include:

Raw reads.
QC and trimming.
Mapping to a reference.
Variant calling.
Filtering.
Annotation or population summary.
Biological interpretation.

Every step can introduce bias. Low coverage, reference choice, population structure, and filtering thresholds all matter.

title: Variants and Population Signals
text:
VCF essentials
A VCF file stores genomic position, reference allele, alternate allele, quality, filters, annotations, and sample genotypes. The header is part of the data: it defines fields and records provenance.

title: Variants and Population Signals
text:
Population signals
Depending on the design, variant data can support questions about:

Genetic diversity.
Relatedness.
Population structure.
Local adaptation.
Candidate genes or regions.

These claims require careful filtering and appropriate sampling.

title: Variants and Population Signals
text:
Project checkpoint
Update your project issue with a four-week plan. Identify the strongest output you already have and the riskiest step remaining.

title: Project Synthesis and Biological Interpretation
text: Week 09 is where bioinformatics results become an argument. Students synthesize evidence, choose the final figure, write caveats, and make the project understandable to another scientist.

title: Project Synthesis and Biological Interpretation
text: Week 09 is where bioinformatics results become an argument. Students synthesize evidence, choose the final figure, write caveats, and make the project understandable to another scientist.

title: Project Synthesis and Biological Interpretation
section: By the end of this week
text:
By the end of this week
You should be able to:

Identify the main claim your data can support.
Separate evidence, interpretation, and speculation.
Choose a final figure or table that answers the project question.
Write a limitations paragraph that improves credibility.
Prepare the final compendium structure.

title: Project Synthesis and Biological Interpretation
text:
From output to claim
Computational output is not the same as a result. A defensible result connects:

A biological question.
A data source.
A documented workflow.
A figure or table.
A cautious interpretation.

title: Project Synthesis and Biological Interpretation
text:
Common synthesis problems
Watch for:

Figures that show workflow success but not biology.
Claims that depend on uninspected intermediate files.
Missing metadata.
Unclear sample names.
Overstated conclusions from weak design.
Results that cannot be regenerated.

title: Project Synthesis and Biological Interpretation
text:
Project checkpoint
By the end of this week, your project should have a complete report skeleton, a candidate final figure, and a short list of remaining fixes.

title: eDNA & Environmental Metabarcoding
text:
Environmental DNA (eDNA) is genetic material organisms shed into their surroundings — water, sediment, soil, air. Sampling and sequencing it lets us detect and characterize whole communities without capturing or even seeing the organisms. In aquatic systems it has become a workhorse for biodiversity surveys, invasive-species early warning, and monitoring rare or cryptic taxa.






Note



This lecture connects directly to the Hyak setup tutorial — the compute-heavy steps below run as SLURM jobs on Klone.

title: eDNA & Environmental Metabarcoding
text:
Environmental DNA (eDNA) is genetic material organisms shed into their surroundings — water, sediment, soil, air. Sampling and sequencing it lets us detect and characterize whole communities without capturing or even seeing the organisms. In aquatic systems it has become a workhorse for biodiversity surveys, invasive-species early warning, and monitoring rare or cryptic taxa.






Note



This lecture connects directly to the Hyak setup tutorial — the compute-heavy steps below run as SLURM jobs on Klone.

title: eDNA & Environmental Metabarcoding
text:
Sampling and contamination control

Collection: filter water (capturing DNA on a membrane), or sample sediment/soil; preserve immediately.
Degradation: eDNA breaks down over hours to days, so detections are biased toward recent presence — a feature for monitoring, a caveat for interpretation.
Contamination is the enemy. Field blanks, filter blanks, and negative controls at extraction and PCR are mandatory. Track them through the whole pipeline; they tell you your false-positive floor.

title: eDNA & Environmental Metabarcoding
text:
Metabarcoding markers
Metabarcoding amplifies a short, taxonomically informative marker across many taxa at once, then sequences the amplicons.



Marker
Typical target group




COI
metazoan invertebrates


12S
fish and vertebrates


16S
bacteria / archaea (and some vertebrate assays)


18S
broad eukaryotes



Marker choice determines what you can detect and which reference database you assign against — decide it before you sequence.

title: eDNA & Environmental Metabarcoding
text:
From reads to biology
The core pipeline, and where each step runs:

Demultiplex & QC — separate samples, trim primers/adapters, filter by quality. (Klone)
Denoise or cluster:

DADA2 infers exact amplicon sequence variants (ASVs) — resolves single-nucleotide differences, database-independent, reproducible across studies.
OTU clustering groups reads at a similarity threshold (e.g. 97%) — coarser, still common. (Klone)

Taxonomic assignment — match ASVs/OTUs to a reference database (BLAST, or classifiers like the RDP/naïve-Bayes approach) to get names. (Klone)
Build the table — a sample × taxon abundance/occurrence matrix: the analysis-ready product. (Raven / laptop)

# Sketch of the heavy step as a Klone job — see the eDNA metabarcoding assignment
module load apptainer
apptainer exec --home $PWD --bind /gscratch/ \
  /gscratch/srlab/containers/srlab-bioinformatics-container-<hash>.sif \
  <dada2-or-cutadapt-command>

title: eDNA & Environmental Metabarcoding
text:
Ecological readouts
Once you have the table:

Diversity — richness and community composition per sample.
Detection / occupancy — did marker X show up at site Y? Occupancy models can separate true absence from failure-to-detect using your replicates and controls.
Community comparison — ordination and differential-abundance methods to compare sites, depths, or time points.

title: eDNA & Environmental Metabarcoding
text:
Caveats specific to eDNA

False positives — contamination, index hopping between samples on a shared run.
False negatives — degradation, PCR inhibition, primer bias against some taxa.
Abundance is soft — read counts are a noisy proxy for biomass; treat quantitative claims carefully.
Reproducibility — record primers, database version, and every parameter. This is exactly the Data Management discipline the course emphasizes.

title: eDNA & Environmental Metabarcoding
text:
This week

Assignment: eDNA Metabarcoding pipeline
Question set: Week 08
Topic hub: eDNA

title: eDNA & Environmental Metabarcoding
text:
References & resources

MarineOmics Portal — reproducible marine genomics, including amplicon workflows.
DADA2 tutorial — the standard ASV workflow.
Roberts Lab Handbook: Bioinformatics Basics, Annotation, Genomic Resources.

title: Self-Directed Skills
text:
The weekly course arc is organized around bioinformatics questions and data types. The tools below are still required, but they are taught as self-directed support skills rather than as the main topic of a week.
Return here whenever an assignment asks you to render a report, work on Raven, submit a Hyak job, open an Issue, or prepare final files.

title: Self-Directed Skills
text:
The weekly course arc is organized around bioinformatics questions and data types. The tools below are still required, but they are taught as self-directed support skills rather than as the main topic of a week.
Return here whenever an assignment asks you to render a report, work on Raven, submit a Hyak job, open an Issue, or prepare final files.

title: Self-Directed Skills
text:
Required before Week 1
Complete the three setup tutorials on the Before Class page:

Your computer: shell, Git, GitHub, project layout.
Raven: RStudio Server and interactive analysis.
Hyak/Klone: SLURM, containers, and first batch job.

title: Self-Directed Skills
text:
RStudio and Raven
Use Raven when you need an interactive R environment that is consistent with the lab’s server setup.
You should be able to:

Log in to Raven RStudio Server.
Clone or pull your course repository.
Open a project from the repository root.
Run R code from a script or Quarto document.
Save figures and tables to output/.
Commit and push changes back to GitHub.

Use the Raven tutorial as your reference when you get stuck.

title: Self-Directed Skills
text:
Quarto reports and slides
Quarto is the default format for assignments and project reports because it keeps code, results, and explanation together.
Minimum expectations:

Use relative paths from the repository root.
Render from a clean session before submitting.
Include enough narrative that another student can follow your reasoning.
Save important output files, not only printed tables in HTML.
Keep figures generated from code whenever possible.

Suggested report structure:

Question or objective.
Data and provenance.
Methods or commands.
Results.
Interpretation and caveats.
Reproducibility notes.

title: Self-Directed Skills
text:
GitHub Issues
Issues are the course workflow record.
Use:

Setup Check before Week 1.
Project Proposal in Week 1.
Weekly Research Progress every week.
Help Request when you are blocked.
Blocker/Bug when you can reproduce a technical failure.

A useful help issue includes the platform, command, error message, what you tried, and a link to the relevant commit or output.

title: Self-Directed Skills
text:
Hyak and containers
Use Hyak when a workflow step is too large, slow, or environment-sensitive for your computer or Raven.
For every Hyak job, save:

SLURM script.
Job ID.
Container path or software environment.
Input and output paths.
Relevant slurm-*.out lines.

Never run heavy analyses on the login node.

title: Self-Directed Skills
text:
Archiving and final compendium
Archiving is not a separate bioinformatics topic week; it is a habit you practice throughout the quarter.
Before the final submission, your repository should include:

A clear top-level README.md.
Scripts or Quarto files used to generate final outputs.
Key output tables and figures.
A data availability note.
Software/platform/container notes.
Links to your project issue and weekly progress issues.

If raw data cannot be committed, document where they came from, how they were named, and how checksums or manifests were recorded.

title: Self-Directed Skills
text:
Fast troubleshooting checklist
When something breaks:

Confirm which platform you are on.
Confirm the working directory.
Confirm the input file exists.
Copy the exact command and error.
Check whether the problem is path, permissions, software, memory/time, or data format.
Open a Help Request issue if you are still stuck.
